coredata.dc:title: Efficient inner filter effect sensors based on CdTeS quantum dots and Ag nanoparticles for sensitive detection of l-cysteine
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Min
Yan, Yujia
Shi, Huanxian
Wang, Cunjin
Liu, Enzhou
Hu, Xiaoyun
Fan, Jun
coredata.dc:description: In the design of new fluorescence sensors, exploration of new sensors with excellent performances and smart designs for the detection of ions and biomolecules is of continuing interest. Therefore, we designed an inner filter effect (IFE) based sensor by effectively combining between CdTeS quantum dots (QDs) and Ag nanoparticles (NPs). Ternary alloyed CdTeS QDs were synthesized by two steps and using 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) as stabilizer, polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) stabilized Ag NPs were prepared by crystal-seed. The emission peak of CdTeS QDs and the absorption peak of Ag NPs could availably overlap by changing the Te/S molar ratios and l-ascorbic acid (AA) concentrations. The CdTeS QDs/Ag NPs (QNs) with an obviously emission at 580 nm were used as IFE-based sensors for l-cysteine (L-Cys). The QNs fluorescence was increasing with addition of l-Cysteine (L-Cys), the fluorescence (PL) intensity (F/F0) was linearly proportional to the L-Cys concentrations ranging from 20 μM to 400 μM with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9942 and the detection limit was calculated to be 0.025 μM (S/N = 3), where F and F0 were the PL intensity in the presence and absence of L-Cys at 580 nm, respectively. Moreover, there was no interference to some potential interfering substances, which showed high sensitivity and selectivity for L-Cys.
coredata.dcterms:subject: IFE-based sensors
CdTeS QDs
Ag NPs
L-Cys

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of PVP stabilized Ag NPs
Procedures for IFE-based fluorescence detection of L-Cys
Construction of CdTeS QDs/Ag NPs IFE-based systems
IFE-based sensors of L-Cys with CdTeS QDs/Ag NPs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province of China
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21476183 and 21676213 ), the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2016M600809 ), the Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province of China ( 2017JM2026 and 2018JM5020 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The UV–vis spectra of Ag NPs with different AA concentrations.
The XRD patterns (a) and FT-IR spectra (b) of CdTeS QDs.
EDX analysis (a) and the PL spectra (b) of CdTeS QDs.
The HRTEM images (a) of QNs and the lattice fringes with the interplanar spacing of 0.32 nm and 0.20 nm (b).
The PL spectrum of CdTeS QDs and the absorption of Ag NPs. The PL spectra of QNs (the molar ratio of Te/S was 0.10:0.75 and 4 mL AA) with different concentrations of Ag NPs.

The PL spectra (a) of QNs with different concentrations of L-Cys. (b) Plots of the relationship between fluorescence intensity F/F0 and L-Cys concentrations. Inset (b): expanded linear region (20 μM–400 μM) of the calibration curve. The fluorescence responses (c) of QNs to L-Cys and other interfering substances. And the effect of response time of QNs to L-Cys on detection results.
The schematic diagram of the CdTeS QDs/Ag NPs for the detection of L-Cys.
Comparison of the performance of different sensors for the detection of L-Cys.

head.ce:title: Efficient inner filter effect sensors based on CdTeS quantum dots and Ag nanoparticles for sensitive detection of l-cysteine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi'an 710069, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Northwest University, Xi'an 710069, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Modern Physics, Northwest University, Xi'an 710069, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest University, Xi'an 710069, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the design of new fluorescence sensors, exploration of new sensors with excellent performances and smart designs for the detection of ions and biomolecules is of continuing interest. Therefore, we designed an inner filter effect (IFE) based sensor by effectively combining between CdTeS quantum dots (QDs) and Ag nanoparticles (NPs). Ternary alloyed CdTeS QDs were synthesized by two steps and using 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) as stabilizer, polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) stabilized Ag NPs were prepared by crystal-seed. The emission peak of CdTeS QDs and the absorption peak of Ag NPs could availably overlap by changing the Te/S molar ratios and l-ascorbic acid (AA) concentrations. The CdTeS QDs/Ag NPs (QNs) with an obviously emission at 580 nm were used as IFE-based sensors for l-cysteine (L-Cys). The QNs fluorescence was increasing with addition of l-Cysteine (L-Cys), the fluorescence (PL) intensity (F/F0) was linearly proportional to the L-Cys concentrations ranging from 20 μM to 400 μM with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9942 and the detection limit was calculated to be 0.025 μM (S/N = 3), where F and F0 were the PL intensity in the presence and absence of L-Cys at 580 nm, respectively. Moreover, there was no interference to some potential interfering substances, which showed high sensitivity and selectivity for L-Cys.

ce:section.ce:para: Thiol compounds have extraordinary significance in biological and chemical analysis and has been used widely in the food and medicine industries [1,2]. At the same time, the study of thiol compounds provides important guidance for the diagnosis of some diseases. Among them, l-cysteine (L-Cys) is a common amino acid in organisms and is one of the sulfur containing α-amino acids [3,4]. L-Cys plays an important role in biological systems [5,6] because it is involved in the process of cell reduction and phospholipid metabolism in the liver and it can protect hepatocytes from damage and promote the recovery of liver function. Mainly used for radiopharmaceutical poisoning, heavy metal poisoning, tincture poisoning and can also be used for hepatitis, toxic hepatitis, serum disease and so on. In addition, L-Cys deficiency is associated with many clinical conditions, such as early atherosclerosis, diabetes, liver disease, Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, and so forth [7–10]. At present, many methods for the detection of L-Cys have been developed, such as colorimetric assay, spectrophotometry [11], capillary zone electrophoresis [12] high-performance liquid chromatography [13,14] and electrochemical techniques [15]. Unfortunately, most of these methods are usually complicated, less sensitive and specific for the detection of target analytes [16,17]. Therefore, the rapid, sensitive and highly selective methods for quantifying L-Cys are eagerly desirable. Li et al. reported a colorimetric assay for detecting L-Cys using gold nanoparticles sensors [18]. A fluorosurfactant-capped gold nanoparticles probe was designed for quantifying L-Cys by Wu et al. [19] However, this system for the detection of L-Cys only under highly basic conditions. Compared with other detection methods, the fluorescent technology possess the advantages of high sensitivity, selectivity and cost effectiveness [20,21], which makes this method is becoming a vital instrument for the filed of biological analysis.

With the development of fluorescence (PL) techniques, all kinds of fluorescence sensing mechanisms [22] have been developed based on the various photophysical processes, including photo-induced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), inner filter effect (IFE) and electronic energy transfer (EET) [23]. Among them, the IFE-based sensors gradually attracted many academicians and experts attention due to the mechanism do without any covalent linking for the fluorophore and the absorbers. IFE is a considerable non-irradiation energy transformation model, which is the result of the absorption of excited or emitted light from the fluorescent carries by an absorber [24,25]. IFE-based sensors can convert the absorption signal into a fluorescent signal to enhance sensitivity and selectivity of the detection [26]. However, the limited selection of absorbents and fluorescent carriers confines the application of IFE-based sensors, because IFE would occur effectively only when absorption spectra of the absorbers overlap with the fluorescence excitation or emission spectra of fluorophores [27,28]. IFE was first reported in 1991 by Gabor and Walt [29], which involving two chemical parties where absorption of one species with the absorption or emission of the other [30–32]. For another, Lin et al. designed a IFE-based visual probe for the detection of tetracycline hydrochloride in milk [33]. In addition, the noble metal nanoparticles (NPs) can be employed as powerful absorbers in the IFE-based sensors, due to large extinction coefficient and tunable absorption spectrum [34]. On the other hand, quantum dots (QDs) can act as ideal fluorescent carriers thanks to their unique optical properties, such as broad absorption spectra, good photostability and adjustable fluorescence emission spectra [35–37].

In this study, we designed a novel IFE-sensor by the effective combination of Ag NPs and the water-solution CdTeS QDs capped with 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA), which was a combined effect of noble metal nanoparticles and QDs. An efficient IFE was instituted between Ag NPs and CdTeS QDs due to the valid overlap of the absorption spectra of Ag NPs with the fluorescence emission of CdTeS QDs. The IFE-sensors was applied to the detection of L-Cys, and the results indicated that the IFE-sensors possessed high sensitivity and selectivity for L-Cys. Moreover, we believe that IFE-sensors could be extended to the detection of a wide of biological analysis and provide technical support for point-of-care-test (POCT).

3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA, 98%) and Tellurium powder (Te, 99%) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent company. Cadmium chloride dihydrate (CdCl2·2H2O, 99%) was provided from China Pine Chemical Reagent Factory (Zhengzhou). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 98%) was purchased from Tianjin Ruijin special Chemical Co., Ltd. Ethanol absolute (analytical regent) was provided from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co.,Ltd. Silver nitrate (AgNO3) was purchased from Guanghua Sci-Tech Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). l-ascorbic acid (AA) was purchased from Tianli SciTech Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Folic acid (FA), glucose, glutathione (GSH), l-cysteine (L-Cys) and dopamine (DA) were purchased from Xiya Reagents. Serine (Seri) and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) were obtained from Shanghai blue season Technology Development Co., Ltd. l-Aspartic acid (APs), l-Phenylalanine (Phe) and l-Alanine (Ala) were purchased from Xi'an Ke Hao Biological Engineering Co., Ltd. Trisodium citrate dihydrate (TSC) was obtained from Fuchen Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin). Thiourea was purchased from Tianjin Yongsheng Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. Citric acid (CA) was purchase from Tianjin KGM Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemical regents were used as received without further experimental purification.

According to our previous report, the synthesis conditions of ternary alloyed depend on the ratios of elements [36]. The ternary alloyed CdTeS QDs were prepared via two steps and using MPA as stabilizer in aqueous solution. Typically, the Cd precursor solution was prepared by mixing 1 mmol CdCl2 and 2 mmol MPA in 50 mL deionized water. Then the pH of mixture was adjusted to 9 by using 1 mol/L NaOH and maintained for 20 min at room temperature under N2 flow. In the meantime, the Te precursor solution was obtained by reacting 0.1275 g Te powder and 0.1135 g NaBH4 in a 50 mL round-bottom flask with 20 mL ultrapure water under N2 flow. After stirring about 10 min, the mixture was heated to 40 °C to gained a clear and purple solution (NaHTe). In addition, the 5 mL thiourea (0.15 mol/L) and given amount of as-prepared NaHTe solution was added the Cd precursor solution under N2 flow. The second was to transfer the as-obtained solution into a Teflon autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 40 min. The CdTeS QDs could be gained after cooling the autoclave to room temperature naturally and the QDs powders were obtained by precipitating the mixture with ethanol and washing with water repeatedly to remove unreacted materials, and finally dried under vacuum at 70 °C for 10 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PVP stabilized Ag NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The Ag NPs were synthesized using crystal-seed method based on the literature [38] with some modifications. In a representative generation of Ag seeds, 0.01989 g TSC and 0.0006 g AgNO3 were added to 33 mL ultrapure water. Under magnetic stirring, 0.6 mL (5 mmol/L) NaBH4 (fisher) solution was added drop by drop into the mixture. Stirring was stopped after 10 min, the Ag seeds were aged at 4 °C for 7 h. The Ag NPs were prepared by using PVP and AA as stabilizer and reductant, respectively. 100 mL of ultrapure water was mixed with 2.0 mL of seeds solution, 2.5 mL AgNO3 (5 mmol/L), 9 mL PVP (0.7 mmol/L) and 9 mL TSC (3 mmol/L), followed aqueous AA (1 mmol/L) were dropped slowly. Under stirring, the color of the mixture changed gradually during the adding of AA and then remained unchanged.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for IFE-based fluorescence detection of L-Cys
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the IFE-based sensors were established by mechanically mixing CdTeS QDs (0.5 mL) and Ag NPs. The equivalent amounts of CdTeS QDs/Ag NPs (QNs) were added into a series of 10 mL colorimetric tubes and L-Cys solution with various concentrations were transferred into colorimetric tube, respectively. Afterwards, the mixture was diluted to graduation lines with PBS solution (pH 7.4) and the PL spectra were recorded after incubating at room temperature for 20 min. Moreover, the same samples by the methods described above to research the selectivity of the IFE-sensors to L-Cys detection, then the solution of FA, AA, Aps, DA, Phe, Seri, GSH, Glucose, Ala and CA were added in each sample in a 10-fold concentrations. All mixtures were reacted 20 min at room temperature and the PL spectra were recorded.

Optical absorption measurements were collected in an ultraviolet and visible (UV–vis) ratio recording spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-3600). Photoluminescence spectra were carried out using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer Record (Hitachi, Japan) equipped with a 150 W Xenon light source, a 1.0 cm quartz battery, a thermostat bath and the slit width was 5 nm. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were taken on a Hitachi H-800 electron microscope at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV with a CCD camera. Fourier transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra of CdTeS QDs were taken by Perkin Elmer Frontier FTIR in the mid IR range with ATR accessory. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) were conducted using a Rigaku D/MAX 3C powder diffract meter (Cu K as radiation source, λ = 0.15406 nm). The energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was recorded with the JEOL JSM-6390A system.

The UV–vis absorption spectra was record to study the optical properties of Ag NPs, and the different absorption bands of the Ag NPs were obtained by adjusting the concentration of the reducing agent AA (in order to reduce Ag ions to Ag atoms), which in the presence of PVP (served as stabilizer), TSC (acted as stabilizer and shape-directing), and Ag seeds solution (provided growth crystal nuclei for the formation of Ag NPs) [39,40]. Fig. 1 showed exhibited the UV–vis spectra of the Ag NPs with different AA contents. It could be clearly seen that there were two evident absorption peak at ultraviolet region and visible region, which were assigned to In-plane quadrupole resonance absorption peak and In-plane dipole resonance absorption peak, respectively. What's more, there was obviously red-shifted for the absorption peak at visible and the absorption intensity enhanced with the increase of AA. This could be attributed to the increasing number of Ag atoms aggregated into silver nanoparticles and the increasing size of Ag nanoparticles with the increasing concentration of AA, resulting in the dramatic red shift of In-plane dipole resonance absorption peak at visible region. The inset of Fig. 1 markedly illustrated the solution color of Ag NPs gradually changed from pale yellow, purple to cyan with the increasing of AA concentrations.

To probe the structure and crystallinity of CdTeS QDs powders X-ray diffraction was performed. As shown in Fig. 2 a, XRD patterns of CdTeS QDs displayed three obviously broad distinct diffraction that could be assigned to the (111)、(220)、and (311) planes of cubic zinc blende, which were agreement with the standard JCPDS data (JCPDS No. 15-0770 for CdTe and JCPDS No. 10-0454 for CdS). Additionally, there was slightly variation that the diffraction peaks of (311) planes slightly shifted towards large angles with the increasing of Te concentrations between pure CdS and CdTe, and there was no extra phase was appeared in the XRD spectra, the results confirmed the phased purity of the ternary CdTeS QDs.

The FTIR spectra were carried out to investigate the group on the surface of CdTeS QDs. According to Fig. 2b, the FTIR spectra of CdTeS QDs showed three obviously characteristic absorption peaks, two peaks at 1400 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1 were the characteristic absorption band of symmetric and anti-symmetry stretching of COOH group, which were agreement with pure MPA [36]. And a broad peak at about 3400 cm−1 was assigned to the OH group of MPA on the surface of CdTeS QDs. Comparing with the characteristic absorption peaks of pure MPA, the distinctly disappearance of SH characteristic peak at 2660 cm−1 illustrated that a covalent band was formed by SH and Cd2+, which showed the successfully modification of MPA on the surface of CdTeS QDs. In addition, Fig. 3 a revealed the EDX picture of CdTeS QDs to analyze the chemical constitutions of CdTeS QDs, and the results suggested the presence of Cd, Te, S, C and O, the C and O in the EDX, which arising from the MPA on the surface of QDs.

What's more, the emission spectra of CdTeS QDs could be tuned by changing the molar ratio of Te/S. As shown in Fig. 3b, the emission peaks of the as-prepared CdTeS QDs shifted from 580 nm to 700 nm with the increase Te/S molar ratios. The significant red-shifted was due to the gradually decrease of the band gap between pure CdTe (the bulk bandgap is 1.5 eV) [41] and CdS (the bulk bandgap is 2.4 eV) [36] with the increase of Te/S ratios.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of CdTeS QDs/Ag NPs IFE-based systems
ce:section.ce:para: The IFE systems of QNs were obtained by mechanically mixing between CdTeS QDs and Ag NPs. The TEM images of QNs were presented in Fig. 4 , it showed that the as-prepared QNs had a fairy uniform size. The darker black dots in Fig. 4a were Ag NPs, and the black areas with lighter colors around them were CdTeS QDs coverage areas. It could be found that the lattice fringes with the interplanar spacing of 0.20 nm and 0.32 nm (Fig. 4b), which were consistent with the (200) planes of the Ag NPs and the (111) lattice planes of CdTeS QDs, respectively.

As mentioned above, the absorption spectra of Ag NPs could be adjusted by changing the concentration of AA and the emission spectra of CdTeS QDs could be changed via regulating the molar ratios of Te/S. Fig. 5 a revealed the absorption spectra of Ag NPs (red) and PL emission spectra of CdTeS QDs (black), which the content of AA was 4 mL (1 mM) and the molar ratio of Te/S was 0.1:0.75. In the QNs system, the effective overlap between the emission spectrum of CdTeS QDs and absorption spectrum of Ag NPs was obtained. In addition, it could be obviously seen that the PL intensity gradually decreased of the CdTeS QDs with the addition of Ag NPs in Fig. 5b, which due to the more emitting light of CdTeS QDs was absorbed when the increase of Ag NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: IFE-based sensors of L-Cys with CdTeS QDs/Ag NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, the PL intensity (F580) versus the L-Cys contents could be fitted to an equation of linear regression with the correlation coefficient of 0.9942 (Fig. 6b). Moreover, the detection limit of L-Cys was calculated to be 0.025 μM by the 3σ/k criterion (S/N = 3, σ represent the standard deviation, k stand for the slope of the linear regression equation). In order to further study the selectivity of IFE-based sensors for the detection of L-Cys, the influence of possible interfering substances were investigated, including: FA, AA, Aps, Phe, DA, Seri, GSH, Glucose, Ala and CA. As showed in Fig. 6c, the response signal of the IFE-based system to these possible interfering substances was no distinct fluorescence changes, only L-Cys induced a dramatic PL intensity enhancement at 580 nm. The investigation indicated that QNs system had highly selectivity for the detection of L-Cys. What's more, the incubation time of IFE-based sensors to L-Cys was investigated and the results revealed that the change of response signal with the prolonging of incubation. The required incubation time was 20 min (Fig. 6d), which the response signal reached the maximum value at this time. In conclusion, the IFE-based CdTeS QDs/Ag NPs sensors showed high sensitivity and selectivity comparing with other sensors for L-Cys (Table 1 ).

In a word, the IFE-based sensors were constructed by the effectively combination of water-solution CdTeS QDs and Ag NPs for the detection of L-Cys. The results illustrated that the quantitative detection of L-Cys was achieved based on the CdTeS QDs/Ag NPs fluorescence signals, the PL intensity of CdTeS QDs/Ag NPs was gradually increasing with the increase of L-Cys concentrations from 20 μM to 400 μM. Furthermore, the study of selectivity showed that the PL intensity of IFE-based system without obviously change to possible interference substances, which indicated that the IFE-based sensors possessed high selectivity and sensitivity for L-Cys. Importantly, we believe that the proposed IFE-based method has good potential for the detection of other ions and biological molecules after further development.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21476183 and 21676213), the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2016M600809), the Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province of China (2017JM2026 and 2018JM5020).
